name: auth_challenge_response
description: MsgBox WebSocket auth handshake end-to-end. Server-produced challenge frame + client-produced response frame (with buildAuthSignedPayload output, Ed25519 signature over it, public key) + server success frame. L3 — round-trip validates the auth_response signature using the public key.

scenario.description: A client opens a WebSocket to the MsgBox relay. Relay sends auth_challenge with {nonce, ts}. Client signs 'AUTH_RELAY\n{nonce}\n{ts}' with its Ed25519 signing key and sends back auth_response. Relay verifies against the DID-published public key and responds with auth_success.

notes: buildAuthSignedPayload(nonce, ts) produces exactly 'AUTH_RELAY\n{nonce}\n{ts}' — three lines separated by LF (0x0A). `{ts}` is rendered as a decimal integer string via JavaScript's default number-to-string conversion.

The payload is UTF-8 encoded before signing. For this vector: 10 bytes 'AUTH_RELAY' + 1 LF + 32 bytes nonce + 1 LF + 10 bytes ts decimal = 54 bytes total. No trailing LF. The test re-derives via Buffer.byteLength and asserts equality with `signed_payload_byte_count`.
`sig` is hex (not base64) per the AuthResponseFrame interface. Implementations MUST emit hex.

`pub` is the raw Ed25519 point (32 bytes → 64 hex chars) that corresponds to the DID's signing key. Relays verify that `pub` matches what the DID's `#dina_signing` verification method publishes — a public-key-substitution attack fails because the PLC doc's publicKeyMultibase (decoded from base58btc) must equal this `pub`.

auth_success has no payload fields — introduced in msgbox 0.14 as a strict-fail-closed success acknowledgment. Older (pre-0.14) relays omit this frame entirely; that transitional behaviour is no longer supported.
